note_data.title: Double entry book-keeping – Part B
keyPoints: **Returns inwards** are goods returned by customers to the business and are recorded as a debit in the Returns Inwards account
**Returns outwards** are goods returned by the business to suppliers and are recorded as a credit in the Returns Outwards account
**Trade discount** is a reduction in the list price given to trade customers and is deducted before recording the transaction
**Cash discount** is a reduction given for prompt payment and is recorded separately in the Discount Received or Discount Allowed accounts
Returns inwards reduce sales revenue and are shown as a **deduction from gross sales** in the trading account
Returns outwards reduce purchases and are shown as a **deduction from gross purchases** in the trading account
**Discount allowed** is an expense to the business and appears on the debit side of the profit and loss account
**Discount received** is income to the business and appears on the credit side of the profit and loss account
When goods are returned, the **original VAT must be reversed** if the business is VAT registered
Credit notes are issued for returns inwards and received for returns outwards to adjust the original invoice amounts

definitions.definition: Goods returned to the business by customers due to defects, wrong specification or other reasons
Goods returned by the business to suppliers due to defects, wrong specification or other reasons
A reduction in the list price of goods given to trade customers, calculated before recording the transaction
A reduction in the amount payable given to customers for prompt payment within the discount period
A document sent to reduce the amount owed, usually issued when goods are returned or an allowance is given

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show the complete double entry with account names, amounts and correct debit/credit sides
Display the ledger accounts in T-account format with dates, details and amounts

modelAnswer.question: Karachi Traders returned goods worth Rs.2,000 to Lahore Suppliers. Show the double entry in the books of both businesses.
modelAnswer.answer:
Books of Karachi Traders (purchaser):
Dr Returns Outwards Account Rs.2,000
Cr Lahore Suppliers Account Rs.2,000

Books of Lahore Suppliers (seller):
Dr Karachi Traders Account Rs.2,000
Cr Returns Inwards Account Rs.2,000
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Returns Outwards debited in buyer's books
Mark 2: Supplier account credited in buyer's books
Mark 3: Customer account debited in seller's books
Mark 4: Returns Inwards credited in seller's books

commonMistakes: Never record trade discount as a separate entry — it must be deducted before recording the net amount
Students often confuse returns inwards with returns outwards — remember inwards means INTO your business
Never show discount allowed as income — it is always an expense in the profit and loss account
Students forget to reverse the entries in both buyer's and seller's books for returns
Never include trade discount in the trial balance — only the net amounts after discount appear
examinerTips: Returns and discounts appear in almost every Paper 2 question
Always specify which business's books you are showing when answering returns questions
Remember: Returns Inwards = Sales Returns, Returns Outwards = Purchases Returns
Cash discount questions often test the difference between discount allowed and discount received
formulas: Net Sales = Gross Sales - Returns Inwards
Net Purchases = Gross Purchases - Returns Outwards
Net Amount = Gross Amount - Trade Discount
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-accounts showing the double entry for returns inwards and outwards, with clear debit and credit entries in both the personal accounts and the returns accounts for buyer and seller
